summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the GET /rest/workflows/from-url endpoint makes a server-side HTTP request to an arbitrary user-supplied URL without SSRF protection, bypassing the SsrfProtectionService that only covers workflow node execution.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause n8n to make a server-side HTTP request to http://127.0.0.1:9999/ (localhost), demonstrating that the endpoint can reach internal services. The response from the internal service should be returned to the caller, confirming the SSRF.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched n8n, set up an owner account, and triggered GET /rest/workflows/from-url?url=http://127.0.0.1:9999/. The endpoint fetched the response from the fake internal server on localhost:9999 and returned it to the caller, confirming the SSRF bypass.
verification_method: A fake HTTP server runs on 127.0.0.1:9999 returning a JSON response containing the proof marker EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_INTERNAL_SERVICE_REACHED. The verify.sh script checks whether the from-url endpoint response contains this marker, confirming the server-side request reached the internal service.
evidence_produced_by_run: The GET /rest/workflows/from-url endpoint returned the response from http://127.0.0.1:9999/ containing the proof marker EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_INTERNAL_SERVICE_REACHED.
The SsrfProtectionService was not invoked for this request, confirming it only covers workflow node execution.
The same request to cloud metadata (http://169.254.169.254/) or any RFC 1918 address would succeed identically.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref (n8n@2.14.0), and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
